knowledges.content: A sequential decision-making setting under uncertainty, where an agent must balance exploration (gathering information to reduce uncertainty about latent parameters) and exploitation (pursuing task objectives). Decisions are made iteratively, with each round producing observations that update the agent's beliefs.
knowledges.label: setup_seq_decision
knowledges.title: Sequential decision-making under uncertainty

knowledges.content: Bayesian Optimization (BO) [@Shahriari2016; @Frazier2018]: a goal-seeking paradigm that selects actions to maximize a reward (or minimize a cost) under uncertainty, typically emphasizing exploitation tempered by uncertainty-driven exploration.
knowledges.label: setup_bo_paradigm
knowledges.title: Bayesian Optimization paradigm

knowledges.content: Bayesian Experimental Design (BED) [@Rainforth2023]: an information-seeking paradigm that selects experiments to maximize information gain about unknown parameters, often without an explicit performance objective.
knowledges.label: setup_bed_paradigm
knowledges.title: Bayesian Experimental Design paradigm

knowledges.content: Active Inference (AIF) [@Friston2010; @Friston2017]: a unifying framework where action selection is cast as a single variational objective—minimizing the Expected Free Energy (EFE)—that decomposes into an epistemic term (expected information gain) and a pragmatic term (expected regret), balanced by a curiosity coefficient $\beta_t \geq 0$.
knowledges.label: setup_aif
knowledges.title: Active Inference framework

knowledges.content: When can minimizing the Expected Free Energy (EFE) guarantee both **self-consistent learning** (i.e., the posterior converges to the true data-generating parameter) and **no-regret optimization** (i.e., the cumulative regret remains bounded)?
knowledges.label: q_central
knowledges.title: Central research question

knowledges.content: If the curiosity coefficient $\beta_t$ is too low, the EFE-minimizing agent becomes myopic: it prematurely exploits, fails to resolve uncertainty about the latent parameter, and the consistency guarantee can be broken.
knowledges.label: claim_low_curiosity_is_myopic

metadata.prior_justification: The myopic-exploitation failure mode is well-attested in the AIF literature and is the dual statement of the sufficient-curiosity floor.

knowledges.title: Low curiosity yields myopic exploitation

knowledges.content: If the curiosity coefficient $\beta_t$ is too high, the agent over-emphasizes information gain, devotes resources to unnecessary exploration, discards the task goal, and incurs catastrophic (large) cumulative regret.
knowledges.label: claim_high_curiosity_overexplores
knowledges.title: High curiosity yields catastrophic regret

knowledges.content:
Building on AIF, [@Li2026] introduces **pragmatic curiosity**, which implements AIF as the acquisition rule

$$\alpha(x|D_t) = \beta_t\, I(s;(x,y)\mid D_t) - \mathbb{E}_{p(y|x,D_t)}[h(y|D_t)],$$

where $s\in\mathcal{S}$ is the latent parameter (the learning space), $h(y|D_t)$ is a problem-dependent potential energy function quantifying the instantaneous regret associated with outcome $y$, and $\beta_t\geq 0$ is the curiosity coefficient that sets the exchange rate between information gain and pragmatic cost.
knowledges.label: claim_pragmatic_curiosity_acquisition
knowledges.title: Pragmatic curiosity acquisition function (Equation 1)

knowledges.content: **Main thesis (the slogan "curiosity is knowledge").** A *single* condition—**sufficient curiosity** (a lower bound on $\beta_t$ that prevents the epistemic term from being dominated by the pragmatic term)—simultaneously guarantees (i) Bayesian posterior consistency in learning and (ii) bounded cumulative regret in optimization for EFE-minimizing AIF agents. Thus curiosity is elevated from an ad-hoc exploration heuristic into an intrinsic regularizer that couples belief updating and decision-making.
knowledges.label: claim_curiosity_is_knowledge
knowledges.title: Main thesis: sufficient curiosity unifies learning and optimization

knowledges.content: **Contribution 1.** The paper proves the first sample-complexity bound for posterior consistency of EFE-minimizing AIF agents (Theorem 5.1). The bound depends explicitly on prior entropy $H_0$, model discriminability $\underline{A}_T$, and the curiosity upper bound $\bar\beta_T$.
knowledges.label: claim_contribution_consistency

metadata.prior_justification: Novelty claim ('first sample-complexity bound for posterior consistency of EFE-minimizing AIF agents'). Plausible given AIF literature, but novelty is always somewhat uncertain.

knowledges.title: Contribution 1: posterior consistency theorem

knowledges.content: **Contribution 2.** The paper proves the first general cumulative-regret bound for AIF with Gaussian Process settings (Theorem 6.1). The bound holds for generalized heuristic estimators and regret functions, and recovers the classical BO-style regret analysis [@Srinivas2009] as a special case.
knowledges.label: claim_contribution_regret

metadata.prior_justification: Novelty claim ('first general regret bound for AIF with GP settings'). Plausible; recovers Srinivas et al. 2009 as a special case.

knowledges.title: Contribution 2: cumulative regret theorem

knowledges.content: **Contribution 3.** The paper translates the two theorems into practical design guidelines for the epistemic-pragmatic balance, hyperparameter selection, and objective function design in hybrid learning-optimization problems, validated on synthetic and real-world tasks.
knowledges.label: claim_contribution_design
knowledges.title: Contribution 3: practical design guidelines

knowledges.content: **Bayesian Optimization (BO) formal setup.** Given an unknown objective function $f:\mathcal{X}\to\mathbb{R}$, BO seeks the input $x^* = \arg\max_{x\in\mathcal{X}} f(x)$ over an admissible query set $\mathcal{X}$, by combining a probabilistic surrogate model of $f$ with an acquisition function that selects the next query.
knowledges.label: setup_bo_problem
knowledges.title: BO problem definition

knowledges.content: **Observation dataset.** At iteration $t$ the available data is $D_t := \{(x_1,y_1),\ldots,(x_t,y_t)\}$, where each observation is $y_t \sim \mathcal{N}(f(x_t), \sigma^2(x_t))$—zero-mean Gaussian noise with standard deviation $\sigma$ around the true function value.
knowledges.label: setup_dataset
knowledges.title: Observation model

knowledges.content: **Gaussian Process (GP) surrogate.** The surrogate model is a GP with joint normal posterior $p(f(x)|D_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mu_t(x), \kappa_t(x,x'))$, where $\mu_t(x)$ is the predictive mean and $\kappa_t(x,x')$ is the kernel (predictive uncertainty). Bayesian inference combines a prior $p(f(x))$ with the likelihood $p(D_t|f(x))$ to yield the posterior.
knowledges.label: setup_gp_surrogate
knowledges.title: Gaussian Process surrogate model

knowledges.content: **Acquisition function.** The next query is $x_{t+1} = \arg\max_{x\in\mathcal{X}} \alpha(x|D_t)$, where $\alpha:\mathcal{X}\to\mathbb{R}$ measures the improvement that the next query is expected to provide. Examples include probability of improvement, expected improvement, upper confidence bound, and entropy search variants.
knowledges.label: setup_acquisition
knowledges.title: Acquisition function definition

knowledges.content:
**Bayesian Experimental Design (BED) formal setup.** Given a parameter of interest $\theta$ (which can be explicit model parameters or any implicitly defined quantity such as a function optimum), BED sequentially selects experimental designs $x\in\mathcal{X}$ to maximize the *expected information gain* (EIG) [@Chaloner1995] about $\theta$:

$$\mathrm{EIG}(x|D_t) = H[p(\theta|D_t)] - \mathbb{E}_{p(y|x,D_t)}[H[p(\theta|D_t \cup (x,y))]] = I(\theta;(x,y)|D_t),$$

i.e., the expected reduction in posterior entropy of $\theta$, equivalently the conditional mutual information between $\theta$ and the next observation pair.
knowledges.label: setup_bed_problem
knowledges.title: BED objective: expected information gain

knowledges.content: BO is *predominantly goal-seeking* (locating an optimizer with few evaluations) while BED is *predominantly information-seeking* (selecting experiments to maximize expected information gain about latent parameters). Although developed historically as separate paradigms, both are responses to the same imperative: act under uncertainty to improve outcomes while reducing uncertainty.
knowledges.label: claim_bo_bed_complementary
knowledges.title: BO and BED are complementary

knowledges.content: **Active Inference unifies BO and BED.** Minimizing the Expected Free Energy yields a single decision objective whose two summands are the pragmatic term (favoring actions that produce preferred outcomes—exploitation) and the epistemic term (favoring actions that reduce uncertainty about latent quantities—exploration). Under this view, exploration and exploitation are not separate heuristics but two aspects of one principle.
knowledges.label: claim_aif_unifies_bo_bed
knowledges.title: AIF unifies goal-seeking and information-seeking

knowledges.content:
**Definition 4.1 (Posterior Consistency).** Let $q_t(s)$ denote the posterior belief over the latent parameter $s\in\mathcal{S}$ after observing dataset $D_t$. The learning process is **(strongly) consistent** if, with probability one,

$$\lim_{t\to\infty} q_t(s) = \delta_{s^*},$$

where $\delta_{s^*}$ is the Dirac measure concentrated at the true parameter value $s^*$.
knowledges.label: def_posterior_consistency
knowledges.title: Definition 4.1: Posterior Consistency

knowledges.content:
**Definition 4.2 (Regret Function).** Let $\mathcal{Y}$ denote the outcome space. A *regret function* is a mapping $r:\mathcal{Y}\to\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ that quantifies the deviation of an outcome $y$ from its desired state $y^*$: smaller $r(y)$ means $y$ is closer to the desired outcome. Special cases:

- When $y^*$ is the global optimum of an objective, $r(y)$ reduces to the conventional instantaneous regret in BO.
- When all outcomes are equally desirable or informative (as in BED), $r(y) \equiv 0$.
- Intermediate cases correspond to hybrid objectives with both performance and safety constraints.
knowledges.label: def_regret_function
knowledges.title: Definition 4.2: Regret Function

knowledges.content: **Definition 4.3 (Potential Energy Function).** Since the true regret function $r(y)$ is generally unknown or intractable, define a time-varying surrogate $h_t:\mathcal{Y}\to\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ that encodes the current model's internal assessment of outcome desirability and is updated recursively as new data arrive: $h_t(y) := h(y|D_{t-1})$. This $h_t$ is non-negative ($h_t(y) \geq 0$ for all $y\in\mathcal{Y}$) and serves as a heuristic estimator of regret conditioned on past observations.
knowledges.label: def_potential_energy
knowledges.title: Definition 4.3: Potential Energy Function (heuristic regret)

knowledges.content: **Cumulative regret.** For a query sequence $x_1,\ldots,x_T$ producing true-function values $f(x_t)$, the cumulative regret over horizon $T$ is $R_T = \sum_{t=1}^T r(f(x_t))$. "No-regret" optimization means $R_T$ grows sub-linearly in $T$ (so $R_T/T \to 0$).
knowledges.label: def_cumulative_regret
knowledges.title: Cumulative regret definition

knowledges.content:
**Theorem 5.1 setup.** The latent parameter $s$ takes values in a discrete space $\mathcal{S}$, with true (data-generating) value $s^*\in\mathcal{S}$. At each iteration $t$, the query $x_t$ is selected by the AIF policy:

$$x_t = \arg\max_{x\in\mathcal{X}}\bigl\{\beta_t\, I(s;(x,y)|D_{t-1}) - \mathbb{E}_{p(y|x,D_{t-1})}[h_t(y)]\bigr\},$$

where $I(s;(x,y)|D_{t-1})$ is the conditional mutual information between $s$ and the next observation pair, and $h_t(y)$ is the (non-negative) potential energy function at time $t$.
knowledges.label: setup_discrete_latent
knowledges.title: Theorem 5.1 setup: discrete latent space and AIF policy

knowledges.content:
**Per-hypothesis observation gap.** For each $s\neq s^*$, define

$$\Delta_s(x_t) := \int_y p(y|x_t, s)\,h_t(y)\,dy - \int_y p(y|x_t, s^*)\,h_t(y)\,dy,$$

the difference in expected energy between hypothesis $s$ and the truth $s^*$ at action $x_t$. This quantifies how much $h_t$ separates $s$ from $s^*$ via the observation distribution.
knowledges.label: setup_observation_gap
knowledges.title: Per-hypothesis observation gap

knowledges.content: **Assumption (i): Finite prior entropy.** The prior $q_0(s)$ has finite Shannon entropy, $H_0 := H(q_0(s)) < \infty$. This means the agent begins with a well-posed inferential task: initial uncertainty over $\mathcal{S}$ is bounded.
knowledges.label: claim_assum_finite_prior_entropy

metadata.prior_justification: Trivially satisfied for any discrete latent space with a finitely-supported prior. The paper assumes discrete S, so finite H_0 is a design choice the user controls.

knowledges.title: Assumption (i): finite prior entropy

knowledges.content:
**Assumption (ii): Observational distinguishability.** Under the observation constraint induced by $h_t(y)$, the true parameter $s^*$ is distinguishable in the sense that

$$\frac{\sum_{s\neq s^*}\Delta_s(x_t)\,q_{t-1}(s)}{\sum_{s\neq s^*}q_{t-1}(s)} \geq A_t,$$

with $A_t \geq 0$ quantifying the average discriminative strength of the current observation. If $h_t$ excessively filters the signal (e.g., focuses only on narrow outcomes), $A_t$ can collapse and the system may fail to identify $s^*$.
knowledges.label: claim_assum_distinguishability

metadata.prior_justification: Holds for well-designed experimental setups but can fail if the heuristic h_t filters out the discriminative signal (paper itself flags this). Moderate prior.

knowledges.title: Assumption (ii): observational distinguishability

knowledges.content:
**Assumption (iii): Sufficient curiosity (for consistency).** The curiosity coefficient satisfies

$$\beta_t \;\geq\; \min_{x\in\mathcal{X}} \frac{\mathbb{E}_{p(y|x,D_{t-1})}[h_t(y)]}{I(s;(x,y)|D_{t-1})},$$

which ensures that the value of information cannot be suppressed by the expected regret penalty.
knowledges.label: claim_assum_sufficient_curiosity_5

metadata.prior_justification: A design constraint: the user/algorithm must set beta_t at or above the floor. If implemented as adaptive scheduling it holds by construction; high plausibility.

knowledges.title: Assumption (iii): sufficient curiosity (Eq. 5)

knowledges.content:
**Theorem 5.1 (Posterior Consistency in AIF).** Under assumptions (i)-(iii) above, define $w_t := \sum_{s\neq s^*} q_{t-1}(s)$ as the total posterior mass on incorrect hypotheses. Then it suffices to run for

$$T \;\geq\; \frac{\bar\beta_T\, H_0}{\underline{A}_T\,\epsilon}$$

iterations to obtain $\mathbb{E}[w_T]\leq \epsilon$, where $\underline{A}_T := \min_{t\in[1,T]} A_t$ and $\bar\beta_T := \max_{t\in[1,T]}\beta_t$. Proof: see Appendix A.
knowledges.label: claim_theorem_5_1
knowledges.title: Theorem 5.1 statement: sample-complexity bound for posterior consistency

knowledges.content: **Interpretation of assumption (i).** Finite prior entropy ensures that the parameter space is *learnable*: the initial uncertainty is bounded, meaning the agent begins with a well-posed inferential task. Without bounded $H_0$ (e.g., a continuum of equally weighted hypotheses), no finite number of queries can drive the expected error mass to a target $\epsilon$.
knowledges.label: claim_interp_finite_entropy
knowledges.title: Interpretation: finite prior entropy guarantees learnability

knowledges.content: **Interpretation of assumption (ii).** Observational distinguishability guarantees that, under the observation model constrained by $h_t(y)$, the true parameter generates statistically distinguishable outcomes from other hypotheses. The condition explicitly couples the *informativeness of experiments* with the *expressivity of the energy function*: if $h_t(y)$ excessively filters the signal, the system may fail to identify $s^*$.
knowledges.label: claim_interp_distinguishability
knowledges.title: Interpretation: distinguishability couples informativeness and energy

knowledges.content: **Interpretation of assumption (iii).** Sufficient curiosity enforces that the epistemic term dominates enough to prevent premature exploitation. When $\beta_t$ falls below this threshold, the policy may repeatedly select myopic actions that yield low expected regret but fail to reduce parameter uncertainty, breaking the consistency guarantee.
knowledges.label: claim_interp_sufficient_curiosity_5
knowledges.title: Interpretation: sufficient curiosity prevents premature exploitation

knowledges.content:
**Convergence-rate interpretation.** Required iterations to reach $\mathbb{E}[w_T] \leq \epsilon$ depend on three quantities:

- $H_0$ (initial uncertainty): larger priors require more evidence to concentrate.
- $\underline{A}_T$ (minimum discriminative power of queries): experiments yielding greater inter-hypothesis differences accelerate convergence.
- $\bar\beta_T$ (curiosity upper bound): higher curiosity encourages richer sampling but may slow concentration, since resources are devoted to wide exploration rather than focused identification.
knowledges.label: claim_rate_dependencies
knowledges.title: Theorem 5.1 convergence rate: three drivers

knowledges.content: **Theorem 6.1 setup.** The unknown function is modeled by a Gaussian Process prior $f \sim \mathcal{GP}(0, k(x,x'))$ with bounded kernel $k(x,x')\leq 1$ for all $x,x'\in\mathcal{X}$, and Gaussian likelihood $p(y|f(x)) = \mathcal{N}(f(x),\sigma^2)$. The query at time $t$ is again the AIF policy $x_t = \arg\max_{x}\{\beta_t I(s;(x,y)|D_{t-1}) - \mathbb{E}_{p(y|x,D_{t-1})}[h_t(y)]\}$.
knowledges.label: setup_gp_model
knowledges.title: Theorem 6.1 setup: GP prior, bounded kernel, Gaussian likelihood

knowledges.content: **Maximum information gain.** $\rho_T$ denotes the maximum information gain achievable after at most $T$ selected points. It controls the size of the GP-side terms in the regret bound and depends on the kernel and $\mathcal{X}$. Constants used in the bound: $\zeta_T = 2\log(m_T/\delta)$ with positive sequence $\{m_t\}$ satisfying $\sum_{t\geq 1} m_t^{-1} = 1$; $C = 2/\log(1+\sigma^{-2})$.
knowledges.label: setup_max_info_gain
knowledges.title: Auxiliary GP-bound constants

knowledges.content: **Standard normal PDF / CDF.** $\phi(z) = (2\pi)^{-1/2}\exp(-z^2/2)$ is the standard normal PDF and $\Phi(\cdot)$ its CDF. Used in Lemma B.4.
knowledges.label: setup_normal_pdf
knowledges.title: Standard normal PDF/CDF

knowledges.content: For $y\sim\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$ the density is $p(y) = (2\pi\sigma^2)^{-1/2}\exp(-(y-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2))$, and the substitution $z = (y-\mu)/\sigma$ gives $p(y)\,dy = \phi(z)\,dz$.
knowledges.label: axiom_normal_density

metadata.prior_justification: Standard textbook identity for the normal distribution under linear substitution.

knowledges.title: Normal-density substitution identity

knowledges.content:
**Assumption (i): Smoothness of task.** The true regret function $r:\mathcal{Y}\to\mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ is Lipschitz-continuous with constant $L\geq 0$:

$$|r(y_1) - r(y_2)| \leq L|y_1 - y_2|, \quad \forall\, y_1,y_2\in\mathcal{Y}.$$

This ensures the optimization landscape is well-behaved so the GP can generalize from observations.
knowledges.label: claim_assum_smoothness

metadata.prior_justification: Lipschitz smoothness is mild for most engineering objectives but can fail for discontinuous tasks (the paper explicitly notes this in Sec 6.3 and recommends deep GPs as mitigation).

knowledges.title: Assumption (i): Lipschitz smoothness of true regret

knowledges.content:
**Assumption (ii): Heuristic alignment.** At each iteration, the discrepancy between the heuristic energy function $h_t(y)$ and the true regret $r(y)$ is uniformly bounded:

$$|r(y) - h_t(y)| \leq B_t, \quad \forall\, y\in\mathcal{Y},$$

where $B_t$ is the maximal heuristic-to-true-regret discrepancy at time $t$.
knowledges.label: claim_assum_heuristic_alignment

metadata.prior_justification: Bounded |r - h_t| holds when h_t is well-designed or learned online. The paper itself flags this as practically challenging (Sec. 9 limitations) -- moderate plausibility.

knowledges.title: Assumption (ii): bounded heuristic-true-regret discrepancy

knowledges.content: **Assumption (iii): Sufficient curiosity (for regret bound).** The curiosity coefficient satisfies the same lower bound as in Theorem 5.1, $\beta_t \geq \min_{x}\mathbb{E}_{p(y|x,D_{t-1})}[h_t(y)] / I(s;(x,y)|D_{t-1})$, ensuring the value of information is not suppressed by the expected regret penalty.
knowledges.label: claim_assum_sufficient_curiosity_6

metadata.prior_justification: Identical inequality to Eq. 5 (linked by equivalence in the formalization). Same justification as claim_assum_sufficient_curiosity_5.

knowledges.title: Assumption (iii): sufficient curiosity (Eq. 8)

knowledges.content: same_truth(A, B)
knowledges.label: equiv_sufficient_curiosity

knowledges.content: **Lemma B.1 (Srinivas et al. 2009 [@Srinivas2009], Lemma 5.3).** The information gain for $T$ selected points equals $I(y_T; f_T) = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{t=1}^T \log(1+\sigma^{-2}\sigma_{t-1}^2(x_t))$, where $\sigma_{t-1}^2(x_t)$ is the GP predictive variance at $x_t$.
knowledges.label: lemma_b1_info_gain

metadata.prior_justification: Established result (Srinivas et al. 2009, Lemma 5.3); peer-reviewed.

knowledges.title: Lemma B.1: information gain via predictive variances

knowledges.content: **Lemma B.2 (Srinivas et al. 2009 [@Srinivas2009], Lemma 5.5).** Pick $\delta\in(0,1)$ and set $\zeta_t = 2\log(m_t/\delta)$ with $\sum_{t\geq 1} m_t^{-1} = 1$, $m_t > 0$. Then with probability $\geq 1-\delta$, $|f(x_t) - \mu_{t-1}(x_t)| \leq \zeta_t^{1/2}\sigma_{t-1}(x_t)$ for all $t \geq 1$.
knowledges.label: lemma_b2_concentration

metadata.prior_justification: Established result (Srinivas et al. 2009, Lemma 5.5); peer-reviewed.

knowledges.title: Lemma B.2: GP concentration

knowledges.content: **Lemma B.3 (Li et al. 2026 [@Li2026], Lemma 3.2).** For any subset $X \subseteq \mathcal{X}$ with corresponding function values $f_X$, given history $D_t$ and new observations $Y$ at $X$, the mutual information satisfies $I(f_{\mathcal{X}}; (X,Y)|D_t) = I(f_X; Y|D_t)$.
knowledges.label: lemma_b3_mi_equality

metadata.prior_justification: Cites Lemma 3.2 of [Li et al. 2026] (companion paper). Mutual-information equality is a standard data-processing-style identity.

knowledges.title: Lemma B.3: mutual information identity

knowledges.content: **Lemma B.4 (Folded-normal expectation).** If $y\sim\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$ and $c\in\mathbb{R}$, then $\mathbb{E}[|y-c|] = (\mu-c)[1-2\Phi((c-\mu)/\sigma)] + \sigma\sqrt{2/\pi}\exp\{-(c-\mu)^2/(2\sigma^2)\}$, with $\Phi(\cdot)$ the standard normal CDF.
knowledges.label: lemma_b4_abs_normal
knowledges.title: Lemma B.4: expected absolute deviation under a normal

knowledges.content:
**Lemma B.5 (Per-step regret bound).** Fix $t\geq 1$. If $|f(x_t) - \mu_{t-1}(x_t)| \leq \zeta_t^{1/2}\sigma_{t-1}(x_t)$, then

$$r(f(x_t)) \leq \beta_t I(f(x_t); y_t|D_{t-1}) + L(\zeta_t^{1/2} + \sqrt{2/\pi})\sigma_{t-1}(x_t) + B_t.$$
knowledges.label: lemma_b5_per_step_regret
knowledges.title: Lemma B.5: instant regret decomposition

knowledges.content:
**Theorem 6.1 (Cumulative Regret Bound in AIF).** Under assumptions (i)-(iii), the cumulative regret $R_T = \sum_{t=1}^T r(f(x_t))$ satisfies, with probability $\geq 1-\delta$ for $\delta\in(0,1)$,

$$R_T \;\leq\; \bar\beta_T \rho_T + L\bigl(\zeta_T^{1/2} + \sqrt{2/\pi}\bigr)\sqrt{C T \rho_T} + \sum_{t=1}^T B_t,$$

where $\bar\beta_T := \max_{t\in[1,T]}\beta_t$, $\zeta_T = 2\log(m_T/\delta)$, $C = 2/\log(1+\sigma^{-2})$, and $\rho_T$ is the maximum information gain at most $T$ selected points. Proof: see Appendix B.
knowledges.label: claim_theorem_6_1
knowledges.title: Theorem 6.1 statement: cumulative regret bound

knowledges.content: **Interpretation of (i).** Smoothness of the true regret function ensures the optimization landscape is well-behaved: nearby outcomes yield similar regret values. This Lipschitz condition allows the GP model to generalize effectively from observed samples.
knowledges.label: claim_interp_smoothness
knowledges.title: Interpretation: smoothness enables GP generalization

knowledges.content: **Interpretation of (ii).** Heuristic alignment guarantees the agent's internal model of regret $h_t(y)$ remains close to the true objective's regret. When the alignment condition holds, optimization steps guided by $h_t(y)$ remain consistent with the underlying objective.
knowledges.label: claim_interp_alignment
knowledges.title: Interpretation: alignment keeps optimization on-objective

knowledges.content: **Interpretation of (iii).** Sufficient curiosity enforces that exploration is adequately weighted to avoid premature exploitation: the agent must not undervalue informative queries. This is the *same* curiosity requirement as in Theorem 5.1, highlighting that a minimum exploration pressure is essential both for consistent learning and for regret minimization.
knowledges.label: claim_interp_sufficient_curiosity_6
knowledges.title: Interpretation: same curiosity floor as Theorem 5.1

knowledges.content:
**Cumulative regret bound interpretation.** $R_T$ depends on three drivers:

- $L$ (smoothness of true regret): smaller $L$ implies a gentler landscape and faster convergence.
- $B_t$ (heuristic-true-regret discrepancy): if $h_t$ is updated to track $r$, $\sum B_t$ shrinks and the bound tightens.
- $\bar\beta_T$ (maximum curiosity): larger curiosity broadens exploration and yields more information gain, but also inflates the exploration term $\bar\beta_T \rho_T$.
knowledges.label: claim_regret_drivers
knowledges.title: Theorem 6.1 cumulative regret: three drivers

knowledges.content: The classical BO-style cumulative-regret analysis of [@Srinivas2009] is recovered as a special case of @claim_theorem_6_1: setting $h_t \equiv r$ (i.e., $B_t = 0$) and choosing $s = f_{\mathcal{X}}$ reduces the AIF acquisition to the Srinivas et al. formulation, and the Theorem 6.1 bound specializes to the classical $\bar\beta_T \rho_T + L(\cdot)\sqrt{C T \rho_T}$ form.
knowledges.label: claim_classical_bo_special_case
knowledges.title: Classical BO regret is a special case of Theorem 6.1

knowledges.content: **Discrete sandbox (Appendix C.1).** Latent state $s\in\{1,\ldots,6\}$, action $x\in\{1,\ldots,4\}$, scalar Gaussian observation $y_t|(s,x_t)\sim\mathcal{N}(\mu_{s,x_t}, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma=0.2$. The mean table $\mu_{s,x_t}$ is constructed so that one informative action yields well-separated likelihoods (average gap $\Delta\approx 1.5$) while the other actions are nearly indistinguishable ($\Delta\approx 0.05$). Energy heuristic $h_t(y) = a(y-c)^2$ with $c=0$ and $a\in[0.15,0.35]$. Posterior error mass $w_t = \sum_{s\neq s^*}q_t(s)$ is averaged over 5 random seeds. Error bars represent $\pm 0.2\,\mathrm{std}$ across seeds.
knowledges.label: setup_discrete_sandbox
knowledges.title: Discrete sandbox experimental setup

knowledges.content: **Figure 1(a) (Prior Entropy sweep).** With $\beta=2.0$ fixed, varying the prior mass $q_0(s^*)\in\{0.7, 0.5, 0.25\}$ (equivalently $H_0\in\{1.09, 1.50, 1.77\}$ as labeled in the legend) shows that smaller $H_0$ yields faster posterior contraction: the curve for $H_0=1.09$ reaches $w_t\approx 0$ earlier than $H_0=1.77$.
knowledges.label: obs_fig1a_prior_entropy

metadata.caption: Discrete sandbox, Prior Entropy sweep at fixed beta=2.0.

knowledges.title: Fig 1(a): smaller prior entropy yields faster contraction

knowledges.content: **Figure 1(c) (Curiosity sweep).** Varying $\beta\in\{2.0, 0.8, 0.05\}$ with the informative gap structure shows that sufficient curiosity is necessary for consistent learning: $\beta=2.0$ and $\beta=0.8$ drive $w_t\to 0$ within $\sim 25$ iterations, whereas $\beta=0.05$ stalls at a high error mass.

knowledges.title: Theorem 5.1 corroborated by Figure 1

knowledges.content: **1D GP bandit (Appendix C.2).** Inputs $x\in[0,1]$ on a uniform grid of $N=200$ points. True objective $f(x) = 0.6\sin(3\pi x) + 0.4\cos(5\pi x) + 0.2 x$, observations $y_t = f(x_t) + \epsilon_t$ with $\epsilon_t\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$, $\sigma = 0.05$. Mutual information is computed in closed form from the GP predictive variance. Heuristic $h_t(y) = |y - y^*| + b_t (y - y^*)$, with bias schedules: aligned ($b_t=0$); constant bias $b_t = 2$; slow exponential decay $b_t = 2 e^{-0.02 t}$; fast exponential decay $b_t = 2 e^{-0.25 t}$. Five random seeds; error bars $\pm 0.2$ std.
knowledges.label: setup_gp_bandit
knowledges.title: 1D GP bandit experimental setup

knowledges.content: **Figure 2(a)+left/center (Misalignment sweep).** With $\beta=1.0$ fixed, comparing aligned ($B_t=0$), constant-bias, slow-decay, and fast-decay heuristics shows that larger and longer-lasting bias produces higher cumulative regret, with the constant-bias trajectory reaching $R_T \approx 50$ while the aligned heuristic stays near zero.
knowledges.label: obs_fig2a_misalignment
knowledges.title: Fig 2(a): heuristic bias contributes additively to cumulative regret

knowledges.content: **Figure 2(b) (Curiosity sweep).** With aligned heuristic, comparing $\beta\in\{6.0, 3.0, 1.0, 0.3\}$ shows that *once curiosity is sufficient*, smaller $\beta$ achieves the lowest regret (e.g., $\beta=0.3$ reaches $R_T \approx 5$ while $\beta=6.0$ reaches $R_T \approx 17.5$); large $\beta$ over-emphasizes information gain and incurs additional exploratory regret.
knowledges.label: obs_fig2b_curiosity
knowledges.title: Fig 2(b): once sufficient, smaller beta yields lower regret

knowledges.content: **Prediction from @claim_theorem_6_1 ($B_t$).** The cumulative misalignment term $\sum_{t=1}^T B_t$ enters the regret bound additively, so larger and longer-lasting bias should produce proportionally higher cumulative regret.
knowledges.label: pred_th6_1_bt

metadata.prior_justification: Direct algebraic reading of the additive sum_t B_t term in Eq. 9.

knowledges.title: Theorem 6.1 prediction: B_t enters regret additively

knowledges.content: **Prediction from @claim_theorem_6_1 ($\bar\beta_T$).** $\bar\beta_T$ multiplies $\rho_T$ in the regret bound, so once curiosity is large enough to ensure validity (the floor from Eq. 8), further increases in $\beta$ should *increase* the dominant exploration term and inflate $R_T$.

metadata.prior_justification: Reading of the bar_beta_T rho_T term in Eq. 9. Subtle: 'larger beta inflates' is true above the floor but the floor itself is also data-dependent.

knowledges.title: Theorem 6.1 prediction: above the floor, larger beta inflates R_T

knowledges.title: Theorem 6.1 corroborated by Figure 2

knowledges.content:
**Constrained system identification setup.** Goal: learn unknown system parameters $\theta$ when valid observations can only be gathered under operational constraints $C(y) \leq 0$. Choose $s = \theta$ and $h(y|D_t) = \mathbb{I}(C(y) > 0)$, giving acquisition

$$\alpha(x|D_t) = \beta\, I(\theta;(x,y)|D_t) - \mathbb{E}_{p(y|x,D_t)}[\mathbb{I}(C(y)>0)].$$

Real-world testbed: 2D plume-field environmental monitoring on a $100\times 100$-unit grid with sensor size $a=1.0$, $\Delta t = 1.0$ s, plume-rate model $R_\theta(x) = R_s/\log(\gamma/a)\cdot\exp(-\langle\theta-x,V\rangle/(2D))\cdot K_0(\|\theta-x\|/\gamma)$ and Poisson likelihood $L_\theta(y|x) = \exp(-R_\theta(x)\Delta t)\,(R_\theta(x)\Delta t)^y / y!$. Saturation threshold $C(y) = y - y_{\max}$.
knowledges.label: setup_csi
knowledges.title: Constrained system identification: plume-field monitoring

knowledges.content:
**Three plume-field tasks (Appendix C.3.2).**

- **(a) Source localization.** Estimate location $\theta=[x,y]$ of a single active source (Source 1; $y_{\max}=60$); 400 hypotheses on a 5-unit grid over $[0,100]^2$.
- **(b) Wind estimation.** Estimate wind vector $V=[v_x,v_y]$ for a known source location (Source 2 with true $V=[-0.3, 0.2]$; $y_{\max}=60$); 400 hypotheses on a 0.1-unit grid over $[-1,1]^2$.
- **(c) Active-source identification.** Determine which of six potential sources are active (true active set: Sources 3, 5, 6, 8 from Sources 3-8; $y_{\max}=30$); $2^6 = 64$ hypotheses.
knowledges.label: setup_csi_tasks
knowledges.title: Plume-field tasks (a)/(b)/(c)

knowledges.label: obs_fig3_csi

metadata.caption: Constrained system identification on environmental monitoring.

knowledges.title: Fig 3: optimal beta scales with task informativeness

knowledges.content: **Design guideline (constrained system identification).** Start with a moderate $\beta$; increase it only when the mutual-information term is consistently small (e.g., due to weak sensor-parameter coupling); stop or decrease $\beta$ once further increases no longer reduce, or start to increase, the estimation error. In practice $\beta$ should be just large enough for the curiosity term to matter, but not so large as to induce unnecessary exploration.
knowledges.label: claim_design_guideline_csi
knowledges.title: Design guideline distilled from Figure 3

knowledges.content: **Prediction from @claim_theorem_5_1.** When $I(s;(x,y)|D_t)$ is small (low informativeness), the sufficient-curiosity floor $\beta_t \geq \min_x \mathbb{E}[h_t]/I$ is *large*. Setting $\beta$ below this floor predicts stalled identification; raising $\beta$ above the floor restores convergence.
knowledges.label: pred_csi_low_beta_stalls

metadata.prior_justification: Inverse scaling of the floor with informativeness follows directly from Eq. 5; high plausibility for the paper's own reading.

knowledges.title: Prediction: floor scales inversely with task informativeness

knowledges.content: **Prediction from @claim_theorem_6_1.** Above the floor, the bound's $\bar\beta_T \rho_T$ term grows in $\beta$, predicting that pushing $\beta$ much higher than necessary degrades estimation/regret performance.
knowledges.label: pred_csi_high_beta_overexplores

metadata.prior_justification: Same logic as pred_th6_1_beta applied to the CSI setting.

knowledges.title: Prediction: above floor, larger beta degrades performance

knowledges.content: **Alternative explanation.** The Figure 3 non-monotonicity could be entirely an artifact of the Poisson likelihood/Bessel-kernel structure of the plume model, unrelated to the AIF-curiosity mechanism.
knowledges.label: alt_csi_kernel_only

metadata.prior_justification: Plume-model artifacts could in principle produce non-monotone behavior, but the same pattern appearing across three structurally different tasks (a)/(b)/(c) with different parameter geometries argues against a model-specific cause.

knowledges.title: Alternative: plume-model artifact

knowledges.content:
**Composite Bayesian Optimization setup.** Multi-objective BO with an *unknown* preference function $g(y)$ that must be learned online. Following [@Lin2022; @Chu2005], $g$ is estimated via probit-likelihood preference queries from a decision-maker. Choose $s = f_{\mathcal{X}}$ and $h(y|D_t) = \mathrm{EFE}(g(y))$, giving the nested acquisition (Eq. 11)

$$\alpha(x|D_t) = \beta\, I(f_x; y|D_t) + \mathbb{E}_{p(y|x,D_t)}\bigl[\gamma\, I(g_y; z|D_t) + \mathbb{E}_{p(g_y|y,D_t)} g_y\bigr],$$

with $x = [x_1, x_2]$ a pair of jointly evaluated candidates and $\gamma, \beta \geq 0$.
knowledges.label: setup_cbo
knowledges.title: Composite BO with online preference learning

knowledges.content:
**Energy-resource-allocation task (Appendix C.4.2).** Distributed Energy Resources (DERs) deployment in Optimal Power Flow on the IEEE 5-bus network (`pandapower`). Inputs $x$ are 40-dimensional, outcomes $y$ are 4-dimensional with components: voltage fairness, total cost, priority-area coverage, resilience.

**Heuristic models compared:**
- Ground-truth preference: $g(y) = a^\top y$ with $a = [1, -1, 2, 1]$.
- Constant-bias $h_0(y) = -a_0^\top y$ with $a_0 = [1, -1, 10, 1]$.
- Learnt $h_1$: query via Eq. 11 with $\gamma = 1$.
- Learnt $h_2$: query via Eq. 11 with $\gamma = 10$.
knowledges.label: setup_cbo_task
knowledges.title: Energy-resource-allocation experimental setup

knowledges.content: **Figure 4(a)+left/center (Heuristic alignment).** Cumulative regret stabilizes (sub-linear growth) only when the heuristic $h$ (and its bias $B_t$) converges. The constant-bias heuristic $h_0$ produces approximately linear regret growth (reaching $R_T \approx 5000$), while the learned $h_1$ (with $\gamma=1$) and $h_2$ (with $\gamma=10$) show their bias decreasing over $\sim 50$ iterations, with $h_1$ achieving lower long-run regret than $h_2$.
knowledges.label: obs_fig4a_alignment
knowledges.title: Fig 4(a): heuristic convergence is necessary for sub-linear regret

knowledges.content: **Figure 4(b) (Curiosity sweep).** Sweeping $\beta\in\{0.1, 1, 10\}$ with a true heuristic shows that sufficient curiosity is necessary for regret to converge, *and* once $\beta$ is large enough to ensure convergence, increasing $\beta$ further incurs larger transient regret before stabilization (the $\beta=10$ curve overshoots before converging).
knowledges.label: obs_fig4b_curiosity
knowledges.title: Fig 4(b): once sufficient, larger beta causes transient overshoot

knowledges.content: **Alternative explanation.** Figure 4's regret patterns could arise from the high-dimensional ($x:40$d) IEEE-5-bus problem geometry rather than from the AIF mechanism per se.
knowledges.label: alt_cbo_random

metadata.prior_justification: High-dimensional geometry is a confounder but does not predict the linear vs. sub-linear regret-growth distinction in Fig 4(a) -- a *functional-form* signature that geometry alone cannot reproduce.

knowledges.title: Alternative: high-dimensional geometry, not AIF mechanism

The constant-bias heuristic $h_0$ produces approximately linear regret growth (reaching $R_T \approx 5000$), while the learned $h_1$ (with $\gamma=1$) and $h_2$ (with $\gamma=10$) show their bias decreasing over $\sim 50$ iterations, with $h_1$ achieving lower long-run regret than $h_2$.)

compare(**Prediction from @claim_theorem_6_1 ($\bar\beta_T$).** $\bar\beta_T$ multiplies $\rho_T$ in the regret bound, so once curiosity is large enough to ensure validity (the floor from Eq. 8), further increases in $\beta$ should *increase* the dominant exploration term and inflate $R_T$., **Alternative explanation.** Figure 4's regret patterns could arise from the high-dimensional ($x:40$d) IEEE-5-bus problem geometry rather than from the AIF mechanism per se., **Figure 4(b) (Curiosity sweep).** Sweeping $\beta\in\{0.1, 1, 10\}$ with a true heuristic shows that sufficient curiosity is necessary for regret to converge, *and* once $\beta$ is large enough to ensure convergence, increasing $\beta$ further incurs larger transient regret before stabilization (the $\beta=10$ curve overshoots before converging).)

**Real-world corroboration.** The two real-world task families—plume-field monitoring (Sec. 8.1) and IEEE-5-bus DER allocation (Sec. 8.2)—jointly support Theorems 5.1 and 6.1 across structurally distinct domains (constraint-driven Poisson observations vs. preference-driven multi-objective optimization).
knowledges.label: claim_real_world_corroborated
knowledges.title: Real-world corroboration of both theorems

knowledges.content: **Limitation: conservativeness.** The sufficient-curiosity guarantees of @claim_theorem_5_1 and @claim_theorem_6_1 are *sufficient but possibly conservative*: deriving tight or adaptive $\beta_t$ schedules remains an open problem.
knowledges.label: claim_lim_conservative

metadata.prior_justification: Self-reported limitation in Sec. 9; derivative of how the bound was proved (union bounds, worst-case constants).

knowledges.title: Limitation: bounds may be conservative

knowledges.content: **Limitation: identifiability and regularity assumptions.** The results depend on identifiability and regularity assumptions (finite prior entropy, Lipschitz smoothness, bounded heuristic discrepancy). They can degrade under model misspecification, nonstationarity, or partial observability.
knowledges.label: claim_lim_assumptions

metadata.prior_justification: Self-reported limitation in Sec. 9; standard caveat for any theoretical guarantee.

knowledges.title: Limitation: dependence on identifiability/regularity

knowledges.content: **Limitation: heuristic alignment in practice.** Controlling pragmatic heuristic alignment ($B_t$) in realistic tasks can be challenging, so the mechanism-focused experiments do not replace broader benchmarking.
knowledges.label: claim_lim_alignment_practical

metadata.prior_justification: Self-reported limitation; consistent with the paper's own Sec. 8.2 results showing learned heuristics outperforming constant biases.

knowledges.title: Limitation: heuristic alignment is hard to control in practice

knowledges.content: The sufficient-curiosity guarantee, while general, is *qualified*: it is conservative (not necessarily tight), assumption-dependent, and vulnerable to heuristic-alignment failures in deployment. These are explicit scope limits acknowledged by the paper.
knowledges.title: Qualified scope of the main thesis

metadata.reason: Equation (5) and Equation (8) are character-for-character identical lower bounds on $\beta_t$. The paper highlights this shared mechanism as the unifying point of the two theorems.

Appendix A proof. Under the AIF policy (@setup_discrete_latent), assumption (iii) (@claim_assum_sufficient_curiosity_5) gives $\max_x\{\beta_t I - \mathbb{E}[h_t]\}\geq 0$, so for the chosen $x_t$ we have $\beta_t I(s;(x_t,y_t)|D_{t-1}) \geq \mathbb{E}[h_t(y_t)]$. Defining the instant regret of expected observation $r_t := \mathbb{E}[h_t(y_t)] - \int p(y|x_t,s^*)h_t(y)\,dy$ and using $h_t \geq 0$ (@def_potential_energy), one obtains $I(s;(x_t,y_t)|D_{t-1}) \geq r_t/\bar\beta_T$. Telescoping mutual information gives $\sum_{t=1}^T \mathbb{E}[I_t] = \mathbb{E}[H(q_0)-H(q_T)] \leq H_0 < \infty$ by @claim_assum_finite_prior_entropy. Algebraic expansion of $r_t$ over $s\neq s^*$ rewrites it as $r_t = \sum_{s\neq s^*}\Delta_s(x_t)\,q_{t-1}(s)$ (@setup_observation_gap), and assumption (ii) (@claim_assum_distinguishability) bounds this below by $A_t w_t$, hence $w_t \leq r_t/\underline{A}_T$. Combining yields $\sum_{t=1}^T \mathbb{E}[w_t] \leq \bar\beta_T H_0/\underline{A}_T$, so by Markov-style averaging at least one $t$ satisfies $\mathbb{E}[w_t]\leq \bar\beta_T H_0/(\underline{A}_T T)$, giving the sample-complexity bound. The derivation is purely algebraic given the three assumptions, so deduction (rather than support) is appropriate. The derivation also directly contradicts the myopic-exploitation failure mode @claim_low_curiosity_is_myopic when $\beta_t$ is at the boundary.

The bound $T \geq \bar\beta_T H_0 / (\underline{A}_T \epsilon)$ in @claim_theorem_5_1 has $H_0$ in the numerator: when $H_0$ (@claim_assum_finite_prior_entropy) is large, more iterations are needed to reach the same $\epsilon$. Finiteness of $H_0$ is what makes the right-hand side finite, hence learnability is the practical content.

$\underline{A}_T$ appears in the denominator of the sample-complexity bound (@claim_theorem_5_1). Since $A_t$ is computed using $h_t$ via the per-hypothesis observation gap, an $h_t$ that suppresses the signal drives $\underline{A}_T \to 0$ and the bound becomes vacuous (@claim_assum_distinguishability).

@claim_assum_sufficient_curiosity_5 is the binding floor under which the proof of @claim_theorem_5_1 fails: the inequality $\beta_t I(s;(x_t,y_t)|D_{t-1}) \geq \mathbb{E}[h_t(y_t)]$ ceases to hold, the agent picks myopic-but-uninformative queries, and @claim_low_curiosity_is_myopic materializes.

Direct reading of the closed-form bound in @claim_theorem_5_1: $H_0$ and $\bar\beta_T$ in the numerator, $\underline{A}_T$ in the denominator, $\epsilon$ in the denominator (so smaller target requires more iterations). The sign of each dependency is purely algebraic.
From @axiom_normal_density: substitute $z=(y-\mu)/\sigma$ in the integral $\int |y-c|p(y)dy$, split at $y=c$, and use $\int z\phi(z)dz = -\phi(z) + \mathrm{const}$. The result follows.

Appendix B proof of Lemma B.5. Sufficient curiosity (@claim_assum_sufficient_curiosity_6) plus the AIF decision rule give $\mathbb{E}[h_t(y_t)] \leq \beta_t I(s;(x_t,y_t)|D_{t-1})$. Since the latent state can be embedded in the whole function $f_{\mathcal{X}}$, $I(s;(x_t,y_t)|D_{t-1}) \leq I(f_{\mathcal{X}}; (x_t,y_t)|D_{t-1}) = I(f(x_t); y_t|D_{t-1})$ by @lemma_b3_mi_equality. Therefore $r(f(x_t)) \leq \beta_t I(f(x_t);y_t|D_{t-1}) + \mathbb{E}[r(f(x_t)) - h_t(y_t)]$. Splitting $r(f(x_t))-h_t(y_t) = (r(f(x_t))-r(y_t)) + (r(y_t)-h_t(y_t))$, the first piece is bounded via Lipschitz smoothness (@claim_assum_smoothness) and Lemma B.4 (@lemma_b4_abs_normal) by $L(\zeta_t^{1/2}+\sqrt{2/\pi})\sigma_{t-1}(x_t)$, the second by $B_t$ (@claim_assum_heuristic_alignment).

Appendix B proof. Conditioning on the Lemma B.2 concentration event (@lemma_b2_concentration), summing the per-step regret bound @lemma_b5_per_step_regret yields $R_T \leq \bar\beta_T \sum I(f(x_t);y_t|D_{t-1}) + L(\zeta_T^{1/2}+\sqrt{2/\pi})\sum \sigma_{t-1}(x_t) + \sum B_t$. By Lemma B.1 (@lemma_b1_info_gain) and the chain rule the first sum equals $I(y_T;f_T)$, bounded by $\rho_T$. For the variance sum, the kernel bound $k\leq 1$ gives $\sigma_{t-1}^2(x_t) \leq \log(1+\sigma^{-2}\sigma_{t-1}^2(x_t))/\log(1+\sigma^{-2})$, so $\sum\sigma_{t-1}^2 \leq C\, I(y_T;f_T)$, and Cauchy-Schwarz yields $\sum \sigma_{t-1}(x_t) \leq \sqrt{T \cdot C I(y_T;f_T)} \leq \sqrt{C T \rho_T}$. Combining gives the stated bound. The argument is purely algebraic given the lemmas, hence deduction is appropriate.

@claim_assum_smoothness directly produces the term $L(\zeta_T^{1/2}+\sqrt{2/\pi})\sqrt{CT\rho_T}$ in @claim_theorem_6_1: smaller $L$ shrinks this term, reflecting the well-behaved-landscape interpretation.

@claim_assum_heuristic_alignment yields the cumulative misalignment term $\sum_{t=1}^T B_t$ in @claim_theorem_6_1, so a tighter alignment (smaller $B_t$) shrinks the bound and keeps optimization on-objective.

@claim_assum_sufficient_curiosity_6 is required for @lemma_b5_per_step_regret (invoked inside @claim_theorem_6_1). Without it, the chain $\mathbb{E}[h_t]\leq\beta_t I$ breaks and the per-step bound (and hence the cumulative bound) collapses; meanwhile the $\bar\beta_T \rho_T$ term explains @claim_high_curiosity_overexplores: too-large $\beta_t$ inflates the exploration term.

Direct algebraic reading of the bound in @claim_theorem_6_1: $L$ multiplies the GP variance-sum term, $\sum B_t$ enters additively, and $\bar\beta_T$ multiplies $\rho_T$. The signs of the dependencies are immediate.

Plug $B_t = 0$ (perfect alignment) and $s = f_{\mathcal{X}}$ into @claim_theorem_6_1; @lemma_b3_mi_equality reduces the mutual-information term to the form used in [@Srinivas2009]. The remaining inequalities match the classical GP-UCB regret bound, modulo our explicit $\bar\beta_T$ which corresponds to the UCB exploration constant.
@pred_th5_1_h0 directly predicts the rank ordering of contraction curves by $H_0$, which matches Figure 1(a) qualitatively.
Even without the theorem's machinery, a more concentrated prior would place more mass on $s^*$ from the outset, naively producing faster apparent contraction.

Both predictions are qualitatively compatible with the curve ordering, but the theorem's prediction (@pred_th5_1_h0) is *quantitative* (linear scaling with $H_0$) whereas the alternative (@alt_h0_entropy_unrelated) merely predicts an ordering.
Theorem 5.1's prior-entropy prediction better explains Figure 1(a).
@pred_th5_1_at predicts that as $\underline{A}_T \to 0$ the bound becomes vacuous, matching the stalled green curve at $\bar A_t \approx -0.013$.
Random seed variability could in principle produce the qualitative spread between curves.
@pred_th5_1_at specifically predicts the *stall* pattern when the discriminative strength becomes negative; randomness alone does not predict the qualitative regime change.
Theorem 5.1's discriminability prediction best explains the stall in Fig 1(b).
@pred_th5_1_curiosity predicts that sub-threshold $\beta$ stalls contraction; Figure 1(c) shows $\beta=0.05$ stalled and $\beta\geq 0.8$ contracted.
Random variability across only 5 seeds could in principle drive the spread.

The qualitative regime change between $\beta=0.05$ (stalled) and $\beta\geq 0.8$ (converged) is *precisely* the threshold behavior predicted by the sufficient-curiosity condition; randomness cannot explain a regime change.
Theorem 5.1's sufficient-curiosity prediction best explains Fig 1(c).
@claim_theorem_5_1 predicts the H_0 dependence observed in Fig 1(a).
@claim_theorem_5_1 predicts the discriminability dependence in Fig 1(b).
@claim_theorem_5_1 predicts the curiosity dependence in Fig 1(c).
Sweeps (a) and (b) vary disjoint design knobs (prior mass vs. mean-table gap structure), giving structurally independent confirmations.
Sweep (c) varies a third independent knob ($\beta$), so the three confirmations are jointly independent evidence for @claim_theorem_5_1.
@claim_theorem_5_1 is the law whose three predictions match Figure 1; the corroboration claim summarizes the inductive support above.
@pred_th6_1_bt directly predicts the rank ordering of regret in Fig 2(a).
Random variability could spread the curves, but cannot explain the magnitudes.
The constant-bias regret growth is approximately *linear* in $t$, matching the additive $\sum B_t$ prediction quantitatively.
Theorem 6.1's B_t prediction best explains Fig 2(a).
@pred_th6_1_beta predicts that, above the curiosity floor, larger $\beta$ yields strictly higher cumulative regret. Fig 2(b) shows exactly this monotone ordering for $\beta\in\{0.3, 1.0, 3.0, 6.0\}$.
Seed variability is too small to reorder the four curves.
The clean monotone ordering of four $\beta$ values across all 30 iterations is a precise quantitative signature of $\bar\beta_T \rho_T$ scaling, not random noise.
Theorem 6.1's beta prediction best explains Fig 2(b).
@claim_theorem_6_1 predicts the additive bias-regret pattern in Fig 2(a).
@claim_theorem_6_1 predicts the beta-monotonicity above the floor in Fig 2(b).
The two sweeps vary structurally independent design knobs (heuristic bias schedule vs. curiosity coefficient).
@claim_theorem_6_1 is the law whose two GP-bandit predictions match Figure 2; the corroboration claim summarizes the induction above.

The non-monotone $\beta$-vs-error relationship in Figure 3 has a low-$\beta$ stall (predicted by @pred_csi_low_beta_stalls) and a high-$\beta$ degradation (predicted by @pred_csi_high_beta_overexplores), matching the joint prediction of both theorems.
The plume model's saturation could in principle produce non-monotone behavior in beta.

The same non-monotone pattern appears across tasks (a)/(b)/(c) which have *different* plume-model details but share the same AIF mechanism, so the AIF-curiosity prediction generalizes more naturally than the model-specific alternative.
Joint mechanism of Theorems 5.1+6.1 best explains Figure 3.

Combining @claim_theorem_5_1 (which gives a floor on $\beta$) and @claim_theorem_6_1 (which penalizes $\beta$ above what is needed) with the empirical sweep @obs_fig3_csi yields the practical moderate-then-tune-up-only-if-needed recipe.
@pred_th6_1_bt directly explains the linear regret growth under constant bias (un-decreasing $B_t$) versus stabilization under learned heuristics where $B_t$ shrinks over time.
High-dimensional geometry is a confounder but does not predict the linear vs. sub-linear contrast.
The qualitative *linear* regret growth under constant bias matches the additive $\sum B_t$ term *exactly*; geometry alone cannot pick out this functional form.
Theorem 6.1's B_t prediction best explains Fig 4(a).
Below-floor $\beta=0.1$ matches @pred_th5_1_curiosity (stall), and above-floor $\beta=10$ matches @pred_th6_1_beta and @claim_high_curiosity_overexplores (transient overshoot).
Geometry is a confounder but does not predict the floor + overshoot pattern.
The two-regime pattern (floor + overshoot) matches the joint prediction of Theorems 5.1 and 6.1; geometry alone does not.
Joint Theorems 5.1+6.1 prediction best explains Fig 4(b).
@claim_high_curiosity_overexplores predicts that pushing $\beta$ too high degrades estimation quality in CSI tasks; this is the high-$\beta$ branch of the non-monotonicity in Fig 3.
@claim_high_curiosity_overexplores predicts that pushing $\beta$ above the floor produces transient over-exploration; this is the $\beta=10$ overshoot in Fig 4(b).
Both real-world tasks independently exhibit the over-exploration regime predicted by @claim_high_curiosity_overexplores.

The two real-world experiments cover qualitatively different problem structures yet both exhibit the floor+overshoot signature of the AIF curiosity mechanism predicted jointly by @claim_theorem_5_1 and @claim_theorem_6_1.

@claim_contribution_design (the practical-design contribution) is instantiated by @claim_design_guideline_csi (CSI recipe), and is validated empirically by @obs_fig3_csi, @obs_fig4a_alignment, and @obs_fig4b_curiosity, which translate the theoretical $\beta$-floor/$\beta$-ceiling tradeoff into actionable rules.

@claim_curiosity_is_knowledge (the main thesis) is established by the four contribution pillars: (1) the proven posterior-consistency theorem @claim_contribution_consistency / @claim_theorem_5_1; (2) the proven cumulative-regret theorem @claim_contribution_regret / @claim_theorem_6_1; (3) the practical design guidelines @claim_contribution_design; plus empirical corroboration (@claim_th5_1_corroborated, @claim_th6_1_corroborated, @claim_real_world_corroborated). The two theorems share the *identical* sufficient-curiosity inequality, which is the structural fact that elevates curiosity to a unifying mechanism.
The three limitation claims jointly constrain the deployment scope of @claim_curiosity_is_knowledge.
